subject: reading
title: Grade 3 Reading — EOG Practice Test 4
instructions: Read each selection carefully. Then answer the questions. Choose the one best answer for each question unless you are told to select more than one.

passages.title: The Stubborn Goat
passages.text:
Part 1

Every morning before school, Lupe led her goat, Pepper, from the barn to the green field behind the house. Pepper was small and gray, with one black ear and a stubborn streak as wide as the river. Some mornings Pepper trotted along happily. Other mornings she planted her hooves in the dirt and would not move, no matter how hard Lupe tugged the rope.

One cold morning, Pepper would not budge. Lupe pulled. She pushed. She even tried pushing from behind, but Pepper only swung her head and chewed on Lupe's sleeve. The school bus would come soon, and Lupe felt her face grow hot with worry.

"Please, Pepper," Lupe begged. "I cannot leave you in the yard near Mother's flowers." But the goat just blinked her yellow eyes and stayed as still as a stone.

Part 2

Lupe sat down on an old bucket to think. She remembered that Pepper always came running whenever the corn bin rattled. Slowly, a plan formed in her mind. Lupe walked to the barn, scooped a handful of corn into a tin cup, and gave the cup a loud shake.

At the sound, Pepper's one black ear shot straight up. The goat forgot all about being stubborn and bounded across the yard toward the field, bleating the whole way. Lupe scattered a little corn in the grass and clipped the rope to the fence post. Pepper munched happily, her tail wagging.

Lupe ran to catch the bus just as it rolled to a stop. She was out of breath, but she was smiling. Sometimes, she thought, you cannot win by pulling harder. Sometimes you have to be a little clever instead.

passages.title: How Maple Crossing Got Its Name
passages.text:
Part 1

Long ago, in the days when folks tell some mighty tall tales, there lived a woman named Big Hattie. Now Big Hattie was so strong that she could lift a wagon with one hand while shaking out a rug with the other. Her laugh was so loud that it shook ripe apples right off the trees, and birds would faint clean out of the sky.

One spring, the rains came down so hard that the river jumped its banks and washed away the only bridge for miles. The people of the town stood on the muddy shore, scratching their heads. "How will we ever cross now?" they sighed.

Big Hattie just rolled up her sleeves. "Step aside," she said with a grin.

Part 2

Big Hattie marched into the woods and wrapped her arms around the tallest maple tree you ever did see. With one great heave, she pulled it up by the roots as easily as a child picks a flower. Then she laid that maple tree flat across the roaring river, from one bank to the other.

"There," she said, wiping her hands. "Now you have yourselves a bridge."

The maple tree was so wide that two wagons could roll across it side by side, and so long that it never once sagged in the middle. The townsfolk crossed back and forth for years, and they were so grateful that they named their town Maple Crossing.

To this day, the old folks say that if you stand very still on a windy evening, you can still hear Big Hattie's mighty laugh rolling through the hills, knocking apples off the trees.

passages.title: What Makes a Thunderstorm?
passages.text:
Part 1

A thunderstorm can fill the sky with dark clouds, loud crashes, and bright flashes of light. Thunderstorms may seem scary, but they begin with something simple: warm, wet air. On a hot day, the sun heats the ground. The ground warms the air just above it. Because warm air is lighter than cool air, the warm air begins to rise.

As the warm air rises higher into the sky, it carries tiny drops of water with it. High up, the air is much colder. The cold air causes the water drops to clump together and form a tall cloud. When the cloud grows big enough and dark enough, a storm is ready to begin.

Part 2

Inside the storm cloud, water drops and bits of ice bump and rub against one another. This rubbing builds up a kind of energy called electricity. When enough electricity builds up, it leaps from the cloud in a giant spark. That spark is lightning.

Lightning is very hot, even hotter than the surface of the sun. It heats the air around it so quickly that the air swells out with a loud bang. That bang is the thunder we hear. Because light travels faster than sound, we always see the flash of lightning before we hear the rumble of thunder.

Thunderstorms do important work. The rain they bring waters crops and fills rivers and lakes. So the next time a storm rolls in, remember that it all started with warm air rising on a sunny day.

passages.title: Garrett Morgan: A Helpful Inventor
passages.text:
Part 1

Garrett Morgan was born in Kentucky in 1877. As a young man, he moved to the city of Cleveland, Ohio, to look for work. He found a job fixing sewing machines, and he learned how all of their tiny parts fit together. Soon Garrett began to wonder if he could build helpful machines of his own.

Garrett was a careful watcher of the world around him. He noticed problems that other people walked right past, and then he tried to solve them. Over the years he invented many useful things, but two of his inventions helped save lives.

Part 2

In those days, the streets were becoming crowded with cars, wagons, and people on foot. Traffic signals had only two settings: stop and go. There was no warning before the signal switched, so crashes happened often. One day Garrett saw an accident at a busy corner, and he decided to act.

Garrett built a new kind of traffic signal with a third setting that warned drivers the signal was about to change. This gave people time to slow down and stop safely. His idea led to the yellow caution light we still use today.

Garrett also invented a safety hood that let firefighters breathe clean air inside smoky buildings. He even used it himself to rescue workers trapped in a tunnel. Garrett Morgan spent his whole life looking for problems to solve, and his careful eyes made the world a safer place for everyone.

passages.title: The Old Apple Tree
passages.text:
Part 1

The old apple tree stands tall in the yard,
Its bark is brown and its branches are scarred.
It has weathered the snow and the summer sun,
And it gives us shade when the day is done.

In spring it wears a soft pink crown,
White blossoms that flutter and drift to the ground.
The bees come humming from far and near,
To sip the sweet flowers that bloom each year.

Part 2

In summer the tree is a tower of green,
The coolest, the leafiest place to be seen.
We climb to a branch and we sway in the breeze,
And watch the whole world through a window of leaves.

When autumn arrives with its red and gold,
The apples hang heavy, a treasure to hold.
We gather them up in our baskets with care,
And Grandmother bakes us a pie to share.

The old apple tree is a friend that we keep,
Through seasons of laughter and seasons of sleep.
It asks for so little and gives us so much—
A gift from the earth that we treasure to touch.

questions.questionText: According to Part 1, where does Lupe lead Pepper each morning?
options: To the corn bin in the barn
To the green field behind the house
To the school bus stop
To the river near the town
questions.explanation: Part 1 begins by saying Lupe led her goat from the barn to the green field behind the house each morning.

questions.questionText: What is the meaning of the word "budge" as it is used in Part 1?
options: To move from one place
To fall fast asleep
To eat something quickly
To make a loud noise
questions.explanation: The text says Pepper "would not budge" and planted her hooves, meaning she would not move from where she stood.

questions.questionText: What does Lupe do right after she remembers that Pepper comes running when the corn bin rattles?
options: She scoops corn into a tin cup and shakes it.
She pushes the goat from behind.
She sits down on an old bucket.
She runs to catch the school bus.
questions.explanation: After her plan forms, Lupe walks to the barn, scoops corn into a tin cup, and shakes it to get Pepper's attention.

questions.questionText: What does the phrase "as still as a stone" help the reader understand in Part 1?
options: Pepper is hiding behind the flowers.
Pepper is not moving at all.
Pepper is very heavy and gray.
Pepper is cold in the morning air.
questions.explanation: Comparing the goat to a stone shows that Pepper stayed completely still and would not move.

questions.questionText: Which word best describes Lupe in the story?
options: Clever
Lazy
Fearful
Unkind
questions.explanation: Instead of pulling harder, Lupe thinks of a smart plan to lure Pepper with corn, which shows she is clever.

questions.questionText: What is the lesson of the story?
options: Animals should never be kept as pets.
You should never be late for the bus.
It is always best to give up quickly.
Being clever can work better than using force.
questions.explanation: Lupe learns that she cannot win by pulling harder and that being a little clever solves the problem, which is the story's lesson.

questions.questionText: Which detail from the text best shows that Lupe was worried?
options: "She was out of breath, but she was smiling."
"Pepper was small and gray, with one black ear..."
"Pepper munched happily, her tail wagging."
"...Lupe felt her face grow hot with worry."
questions.explanation: The line stating that Lupe "felt her face grow hot with worry" directly shows she was worried.

questions.questionText: Select the two things Lupe tries before her plan with the corn works.
options: She ties Pepper to the fence post.
She begs Pepper to move.
She shakes a cup of corn.
She pulls and pushes the rope.
questions.explanation: Before the corn plan, Lupe pulls and pushes Pepper and begs her to move. Shaking the corn and tying her to the post happen as part of the successful plan.

questions.questionText: According to Part 1, what washes away the only bridge for miles?
options: A falling maple tree
Big Hattie's loud laugh
The river flooding over its banks
A strong wind
questions.explanation: Part 1 says the rains came down so hard that the river jumped its banks and washed away the bridge.

questions.questionText: This story is a tall tale. Which detail best shows that it is a tall tale rather than a true story?
options: The townsfolk stand on the muddy shore.
Big Hattie pulls up a giant maple tree by the roots with her arms.
Big Hattie rolls up her sleeves.
The people name their town Maple Crossing.
questions.explanation: Pulling up a giant tree by its roots with bare arms is impossible and exaggerated, which is a sign of a tall tale.

questions.questionText: What does the word "heave" mean as it is used in Part 2?
options: A strong pull or lift
A gentle whisper
A quiet rest
A loud laugh
questions.explanation: Big Hattie pulls the tree up "with one great heave," meaning she uses one strong pull or lift.

questions.questionText: Why do the townsfolk name their town Maple Crossing?
options: Many maple trees grow all around the town.
Big Hattie was also known as Maple.
They cross the river on Big Hattie's maple-tree bridge.
Maple syrup is made in the town each spring.
questions.explanation: The town is named for the maple tree Big Hattie laid across the river, which the townsfolk cross for years.

questions.questionText: What does the phrase "her laugh was so loud that it shook ripe apples right off the trees" help the reader understand about Big Hattie?
options: She liked to eat many apples.
She worked picking apples for a living.
She had a powerful, enormous laugh.
She was afraid of loud noises.
questions.explanation: Saying her laugh shook apples off the trees is an exaggeration that shows how powerful and huge her laugh was.

questions.questionText: Which word best describes Big Hattie?
options: Worried
Shy
Selfish
Confident
questions.explanation: Big Hattie grins, says "Step aside," and solves the problem with ease, which shows she is confident.

questions.questionText: Use both parts of this text to answer this question. What is the main message of the story?
options: A strong, willing helper can solve a big problem for a whole town.
Maple trees make the best kind of bridges.
Towns should always be built near rivers.
People should never travel during a storm.
questions.explanation: Across both parts, Big Hattie steps in and uses her strength to give the whole town a way to cross, showing how one willing helper can solve a big problem.

questions.questionText: How wide is the maple-tree bridge that Big Hattie makes?
options: Wide enough for two wagons to roll side by side
Only wide enough for the birds
Too narrow for anyone to cross
Wide enough for one person to walk across
questions.explanation: Part 2 states the maple tree was so wide that two wagons could roll across it side by side.

questions.questionText: According to Part 1, what does the sun heat first to begin a thunderstorm?
options: The cold air
The rain
The ground
The clouds high in the sky
questions.explanation: Part 1 explains that the sun heats the ground, and the ground then warms the air just above it.

questions.questionText: Why does the warm air begin to rise into the sky?
options: Rain pulls the air higher.
The clouds are too heavy to stay down.
Warm air is lighter than cool air.
Lightning pushes the air upward.
questions.explanation: The text states that because warm air is lighter than cool air, the warm air begins to rise.

questions.questionText: What step happens right after enough electricity builds up inside the storm cloud?
options: The warm air begins to rise.
A giant spark leaps from the cloud as lightning.
The water drops clump into a tall cloud.
The sun heats the ground.
questions.explanation: Part 2 says when enough electricity builds up, it leaps from the cloud in a giant spark, which is lightning.

questions.questionText: What is the meaning of the word "swells" as it is used in Part 2?
options: Grows quiet
Pushes out and grows larger
Falls toward the ground
Cools down slowly
questions.explanation: The air "swells out with a loud bang," meaning it pushes out and grows larger very quickly.

questions.questionText: How does the author connect the ideas in Part 1 and Part 2 of the text?
options: By telling a story about one rainy day
By describing the steps that lead to thunder and lightning, in order
By comparing two different kinds of storms
By listing reasons people should fear storms
questions.explanation: Part 1 explains how the storm cloud forms and Part 2 continues with how lightning and thunder happen, giving the steps of a thunderstorm in order.

questions.questionText: What is the connection between lightning and thunder, according to Part 2?
options: Lightning heats the air so fast that it makes the thunder sound.
Thunder causes lightning to form.
Lightning and thunder happen for completely separate reasons.
Thunder cools the air and creates lightning.
questions.explanation: The text shows cause and effect: lightning heats the air so quickly that it swells out with a loud bang, which is the thunder.

questions.questionText: What is the main idea of the text?
options: Thunderstorms are too dangerous to study.
Rain is the only useful part of a storm.
Thunderstorms form through a series of steps that begin with warm air rising.
Lightning is hotter than the surface of the sun.
questions.explanation: The whole text explains how thunderstorms form step by step, starting with warm air rising on a sunny day.

questions.questionText: Which statement from the text best supports the idea that thunderstorms are helpful?
options: "The rain they bring waters crops and fills rivers and lakes."
"That bang is the thunder we hear."
"Lightning is very hot, even hotter than the surface of the sun."
"Thunderstorms may seem scary..."
questions.explanation: The line about rain watering crops and filling rivers and lakes supports the idea that thunderstorms do important, helpful work.

questions.questionText: According to Part 1, what was Garrett Morgan's first job in Cleveland?
options: Fixing sewing machines
Building traffic signals
Driving wagons
Fighting fires
questions.explanation: Part 1 says Garrett found a job fixing sewing machines and learned how their parts fit together.

questions.questionText: What made Garrett decide to build a new kind of traffic signal?
options: His boss told him to build one.
He saw an accident at a busy corner.
He wanted to win a prize.
He read about signals in a book.
questions.explanation: Part 2 says one day Garrett saw an accident at a busy corner, and he decided to act.

questions.questionText: What is the meaning of the word "caution" as it is used in Part 2?
options: Speed and hurry
Care and warning
Anger and noise
Rest and sleep
questions.explanation: The yellow caution light warns drivers the signal is about to change, so "caution" means care and warning.

questions.questionText: What is the main idea of the text?
options: Sewing machines have many tiny parts.
Garrett Morgan was born in Kentucky in 1877.
Old traffic signals had only two settings.
Garrett Morgan was an inventor who solved problems to keep people safe.
questions.explanation: The text as a whole shows that Garrett Morgan noticed problems and invented things, like the caution light and safety hood, that kept people safe.

questions.questionText: How does the author connect the ideas in Part 2 about the traffic signal?
options: By listing the years Garrett worked
By comparing two cities Garrett lived in
By describing what a sewing machine looks like
By presenting a problem and the solution Garrett created
questions.explanation: Part 2 first describes the problem of dangerous signals with no warning, then explains Garrett's solution: a third setting that warned drivers.

questions.questionText: Select the two inventions described in the text that helped save lives.
options: A faster kind of wagon
A safety hood for breathing clean air
A sewing machine
A traffic signal with a warning setting
questions.explanation: The text says two of Garrett's inventions helped save lives: the improved traffic signal and the safety hood that let firefighters breathe clean air.

questions.questionText: Which word best describes Garrett Morgan, based on the text?
options: Fearful
Careless
Observant
Forgetful
questions.explanation: The text says Garrett was a careful watcher who noticed problems others walked past, which shows he was observant.

questions.questionText: According to the text, what did Garrett's safety hood allow firefighters to do?
options: See clearly in the dark
Lift heavy objects more easily
Breathe clean air inside smoky buildings
Drive through busy streets safely
questions.explanation: Part 2 states the safety hood let firefighters breathe clean air inside smoky buildings.

questions.questionText: According to Part 1, what does the apple tree give in spring?
options: Red and gold leaves
Baskets of ripe apples
A pie to share
Soft pink and white blossoms
questions.explanation: Part 1 says in spring the tree wears a soft pink crown of white blossoms that flutter and drift to the ground.

questions.questionText: What does the poet mean by the phrase "a window of leaves" in Part 2?
options: A picture of leaves hanging in the house
Leaves that are made of glass
A real glass window on the tree
Looking out at the world through the leafy branches
questions.explanation: From up in the tree, the children watch the world through the leaves, so "a window of leaves" means looking out through the leafy branches.

questions.questionText: In Part 2, the line "the coolest, the leafiest place to be seen" helps the reader picture the tree in which season?
options: Summer
Winter
Autumn
Spring
questions.explanation: The line appears in the stanza that begins "In summer the tree is a tower of green," describing the tree in summer.

questions.questionText: What is the main message of the poem?
options: Apple trees are hard to take care of.
The apple tree is a treasured friend that gives gifts through all the seasons.
Autumn is the best season of the year.
Children should not climb trees.
questions.explanation: The poem describes the tree's gifts in every season and calls it a friend the family treasures, which is its main message.

questions.questionText: How is the poem mainly organized?
options: By giving directions for planting a tree
By telling about the tree through the four seasons
By describing a problem and a solution
By comparing two different trees
questions.explanation: The poem moves through spring, summer, and autumn, describing the apple tree across the seasons.

questions.questionText: Which line from the poem best shows that the family loves the tree?
options: "The bees come humming from far and near,"
"When autumn arrives with its red and gold,"
"The old apple tree is a friend that we keep,"
"Its bark is brown and its branches are scarred."
questions.explanation: Calling the tree "a friend that we keep" shows the family's love for it.

questions.questionText: What does the family do with the apples they gather in autumn?
options: They sell them at a market.
They plant them to grow new trees.
They give them all to the bees.
Grandmother bakes them into a pie to share.
questions.explanation: Part 2 says they gather the apples and Grandmother bakes them a pie to share.

questions.questionText: Both "The Stubborn Goat" and "The Old Apple Tree" share which idea?
options: A strong helper saves a whole town.
A storm changes everyone's plans.
A clever trick solves a hard problem.
Something in nature is valued by the people in the text.
questions.explanation: In both texts, something from nature is valued: Lupe cares for her goat, and the family treasures the apple tree. The other choices fit only one text or another in the set.
